G746.full: (a) rule (kingly or magisterial), (b) plur: in a quasi-personal sense, almost: rulers, magistrates, (c) beginning.

G2098.full: the good news of the coming of the Messiah, the gospel; the gen. after it expresses sometimes the giver (God), sometimes the subject (the Messiah, etc.), sometimes the human transmitter (an apostle).

G2424.full: Jesus; the Greek form of Joshua; Jesus, son of Eliezer; Jesus, surnamed Justus.

G2531.full: according to the manner in which, in the degree that, just as, as.

G1125.full: I write; pass: it is written, it stands written (in the scriptures).

G4396.full: a prophet, poet; a person gifted at expositing divine truth.

G649.brief: I send forth, send, send away, dismiss
G649.full: I send forth, send (as a messenger, commission, etc.), send away, dismiss.

G32.full: a messenger, generally a (supernatural) messenger from God, an angel, conveying news or behests from God to men.

G4253.full: (a) of place: before, in front of, (b) of time: before, earlier than.

G2680.full: I build, construct, prepare, make ready.

G5456.full: a sound, noise, voice, language, dialect.

G2048.full: as an adj: deserted, desolate, waste; hence: the desert, to the east and south of Palestine; of a person: deserted, abandoned, desolate.

G2117.full: adj: (a) straight of direction, as opposed to crooked, (b) upright; adv: immediately.

G4160.full: (a) I make, manufacture, construct, (b) I do, act, cause.

G1096.full: I come into being, am born, become, come about, happen.

G907.full: lit: I dip, submerge, but specifically of ceremonial dipping; I baptize.

G1519.full: into, in, unto, to, upon, towards, for, among.

G859.full: a sending away, a letting go, a release, pardon, complete forgiveness.

G266.full: prop: missing the mark; hence: (a) guilt, sin, (b) a fault, failure (in an ethical sense), sinful deed.

G1607.full: I depart from; I am voided, cast out; I proceed from, am spoken; I burst forth, flow out, am spread abroad.

G3956.full: all, the whole, every kind of.

G2449.full: Judea, a Roman province, capital Jerusalem.

G5561.full: (a) a country or region, (b) the land, as opposed to the sea, (c) the country, distinct from town, (d) plur: fields.

G2446.full: the Jordan, a great river flowing due south and bounding Galilee, Samaria, and Judea on the east.

G1843.full: (a) I consent fully, agree out and out, (b) I confess, admit, acknowledge (cf. the early Hellenistic sense of the middle: I acknowledge a debt), (c) I give thanks, praise.

G2359.full: hair (of the head or of animals).

G2223.full: a girdle, belt, waistband; because the purse was kept there, also: a purse.

G2068.full: I eat, partake of food; met: I devour, consume (e.g. as rust does).

G3004.full: (denoting speech in progress), (a) I say, speak; I mean, mention, tell, (b) I call, name, especially in the pass., (c) I tell, command.

G2478.full: strong (originally and generally of physical strength); mighty, powerful, vehement, sure.

G2425.full: (a) considerable, sufficient, of number, quantity, time, (b) of persons: sufficiently strong (good, etc.), worthy, suitable, with various constructions, (c) many, much.

G2955.full: I bend, stoop down, bow the head.

G3089.full: (a) I loose, untie, release, (b) met: I break, destroy, set at naught, contravene; I break up a meeting, annul.

G2438.full: a thong, strap, (a) for binding a man who is to be flogged, (b) for fastening a sandal or shoe.

G1161.brief: but, on the other hand, and

G40.full: set apart by (or for) God, holy, sacred.

G2250.full: a day, the period from sunrise to sunset.

G3478.full: Nazareth, a city of Galilee, where Jesus lived before His ministry.

G1056.full: Galilee, a district towards the southern end of the Roman province Syria; the northern division of Palestine.

G305.full: I go up, mount, ascend; of things: I rise, spring up, come up.

G1537.brief: from out, out from among, from
G1537.full: from out, out from among, from, suggesting from the interior outwards.

G3772.full: heaven, (a) the visible heavens: the atmosphere, the sky, the starry heavens, (b) the spiritual heavens.

G5613.brief: as, like as, how, while, so that

G27.full: loved, beloved, with two special applications: the Beloved, a title of the Messiah (Christ), as beloved beyond all others by the God who sent Him; of Christians, as beloved by God, Christ, and one another.

G3860.full: I hand over, pledge, hand down, deliver, commit, commend, betray, abandon.

G3754.full: that, since, because; may introduce direct discourse.

G2540.full: fitting season, season, opportunity, occasion, time.

G1448.full: trans: I bring near; intrans: I come near, approach.

G4100.full: I believe, have faith in, trust in; pass: I am entrusted with.

G3855.full: I pass by, depart, pass away.

G3844.full: gen: from; dat: beside, in the presence of; acc: alongside of.

G2281.full: (a) the sea, in contrast to the land, (b) a particular sea or lake, e.g. the sea of Galilee (Tiberias), the Red Sea.

G80.full: a brother, member of the same religious community, especially a fellow-Christian.

G1205.full: come hither, come, hither, an exclamatory word.

G444.brief: a man, one of the human race

G1849.full: (a) power, authority, weight, especially: moral authority, influence, (b) in a quasi-personal sense, derived from later Judaism, of a spiritual power, and hence of an earthly power.

G1122.full: (a) in Jerusalem, a scribe, one learned in the Jewish Law, a religious teacher, (b) at Ephesus, the town-clerk, the secretary of the city, (c) a man of learning generally.

G622.full: (a) I kill, destroy, (b) I lose, mid: I am perishing (the resultant death being viewed as certain).

G2008.full: (a) I rebuke, chide, admonish, (b) I warn.

bsb.1: This is the beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God.
bsb.2: As it is written in Isaiah the prophet: “Behold, I will send My messenger ahead of You, who will prepare Your way.”
bsb.3: “A voice of one calling in the wilderness, ‘Prepare the way for the Lord, make straight paths for Him.’”
bsb.4: John the Baptist appeared in the wilderness, preaching a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins.
bsb.5: People went out to him from all of Jerusalem and the countryside of Judea. Confessing their sins, they were baptized by him in the Jordan River.
bsb.6: John was clothed in camel’s hair, with a leather belt around his waist. His food was locusts and wild honey.
bsb.7: And he proclaimed: “After me will come One more powerful than I, the straps of whose sandals I am not worthy to stoop down and untie.
bsb.8: I baptize you with water, but He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit. ”
bsb.9: In those days Jesus came from Nazareth in Galilee and was baptized by John in the Jordan.
bsb.10: As soon as Jesus came up out of the water, He saw the heavens breaking open and the Spirit descending on Him like a dove.
bsb.11: And a voice came from heaven: “You are My beloved Son; in You I am well pleased.”
bsb.12: At once the Spirit drove Jesus into the wilderness,
bsb.13: and He was there for forty days, being tempted by Satan. He was with the wild animals, and the angels ministered to Him.
bsb.14: After the arrest of John, Jesus went into Galilee and proclaimed the gospel of God.
bsb.15: “The time is fulfilled,” He said, “and the kingdom of God is near. Repent and believe in the gospel!”
bsb.16: As Jesus was walking beside the Sea of Galilee, He saw Simon and his brother Andrew. They were casting a net into the sea, for they were fishermen.
bsb.17: “Come, follow Me,” Jesus said, “and I will make you fishers of men.”
bsb.18: And at once they left their nets and followed Him.
bsb.19: Going on a little farther, He saw James son of Zebedee and his brother John. They were in a boat, mending their nets.
bsb.20: Immediately Jesus called them, and they left their father Zebedee in the boat with the hired men and followed Him.
bsb.21: Then Jesus and His companions went to Capernaum, and right away Jesus entered the synagogue on the Sabbath and began to teach.
bsb.22: The people were astonished at His teaching, because He taught as one who had authority, and not as the scribes.
bsb.23: Suddenly a man with an unclean spirit cried out in the synagogue:
bsb.24: “What do You want with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have You come to destroy us? I know who You are—the Holy One of God!”
bsb.25: But Jesus rebuked the spirit. “Be silent!” He said. “Come out of him!”
bsb.26: At this, the unclean spirit threw the man into convulsions and came out with a loud shriek.
bsb.27: All the people were amazed and began to ask one another, “What is this? A new teaching with authority! He commands even the unclean spirits, and they obey Him!”
bsb.28: And the news about Jesus spread quickly through the whole region of Galilee.
bsb.29: As soon as Jesus and His companions had left the synagogue, they went with James and John to the home of Simon and Andrew.
bsb.30: Simon’s mother-in-law was sick in bed with a fever, and they promptly told Jesus about her.
bsb.31: So He went to her, took her by the hand, and helped her up. The fever left her, and she began to serve them.
bsb.32: That evening, after sunset, people brought to Jesus all the sick and demon-possessed,
bsb.33: and the whole town gathered at the door.
bsb.34: And He healed many who were ill with various diseases and drove out many demons. But He would not allow the demons to speak, because they knew who He was.
bsb.35: Early in the morning, while it was still dark, Jesus got up and went out to a solitary place to pray.
bsb.36: Simon and his companions went to look for Him,
bsb.37: and when they found Him, they said, “Everyone is looking for You!”
bsb.38: But Jesus answered, “Let us go on to the neighboring towns so I can preach there as well, for that is why I have come.”
bsb.39: So He went throughout Galilee, preaching in their synagogues and driving out demons.
bsb.40: Then a leper came to Jesus, begging on his knees: “If You are willing, You can make me clean.”
bsb.41: Moved with compassion, Jesus reached out His hand and touched the man. “I am willing,” He said. “Be clean!”
bsb.42: And immediately the leprosy left him, and the man was cleansed.
bsb.43: Jesus promptly sent him away with a stern warning:
bsb.44: “See that you don’t tell anyone. But go, show yourself to the priest and present the offering Moses prescribed for your cleansing, as a testimony to them.”
bsb.45: But the man went out and openly began to proclaim and spread the news. Consequently, Jesus could no longer enter a town in plain view, but He stayed out in solitary places. Yet people came to Him from every quarter.

web.1: The beginning of the Good News of Jesus Christ, the Son of God.
web.2: As it is written in the prophets, “Behold, I send my messenger before your face, who will prepare your way before you:
web.3: the voice of one crying in the wilderness, ‘Make ready the way of the Lord! Make his paths straight!’”
web.4: John came baptizing in the wilderness and preaching the baptism of repentance for forgiveness of sins.
web.5: All the country of Judea and all those of Jerusalem went out to him. They were baptized by him in the Jordan river, confessing their sins.
web.6: John was clothed with camel’s hair and a leather belt around his waist. He ate locusts and wild honey.
web.7: He preached, saying, “After me comes he who is mightier than I, the thong of whose sandals I am not worthy to stoop down and loosen.
web.8: I baptized you in water, but he will baptize you in the Holy Spirit.”
web.9: In those days, Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee, and was baptized by John in the Jordan.
web.10: Immediately coming up from the water, he saw the heavens parting, and the Spirit descending on him like a dove.
web.11: A voice came out of the sky, “You are my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.”
web.12: Immediately the Spirit drove him out into the wilderness.
web.13: He was there in the wilderness forty days tempted by Satan. He was with the wild animals; and the angels were serving him.
web.14: Now after John was taken into custody, Jesus came into Galilee, preaching the Good News of God’s Kingdom,
web.15: and saying, “The time is fulfilled, and God’s Kingdom is at hand! Repent, and believe in the Good News.”
web.16: Passing along by the sea of Galilee, he saw Simon and Andrew the brother of Simon casting a net into the sea, for they were fishermen.
web.17: Jesus said to them, “Come after me, and I will make you into fishers for men.”
web.18: Immediately they left their nets, and followed him.
web.19: Going on a little further from there, he saw James the son of Zebedee, and John, his brother, who were also in the boat mending the nets.
web.20: Immediately he called them, and they left their father, Zebedee, in the boat with the hired servants, and went after him.
web.21: They went into Capernaum, and immediately on the Sabbath day he entered into the synagogue and taught.
web.22: They were astonished at his teaching, for he taught them as having authority, and not as the scribes.
web.23: Immediately there was in their synagogue a man with an unclean spirit, and he cried out,
web.24: saying, “Ha! What do we have to do with you, Jesus, you Nazarene? Have you come to destroy us? I know you who you are: the Holy One of God!”
web.25: Jesus rebuked him, saying, “Be quiet, and come out of him!”
web.26: The unclean spirit, convulsing him and crying with a loud voice, came out of him.
web.27: They were all amazed, so that they questioned among themselves, saying, “What is this? A new teaching? For with authority he commands even the unclean spirits, and they obey him!”
web.28: The report of him went out immediately everywhere into all the region of Galilee and its surrounding area.
web.29: Immediately, when they had come out of the synagogue, they came into the house of Simon and Andrew, with James and John.
web.30: Now Simon’s wife’s mother lay sick with a fever, and immediately they told him about her.
web.31: He came and took her by the hand, and raised her up. The fever left her, and she served them.
web.32: At evening, when the sun had set, they brought to him all who were sick, and those who were possessed by demons.
web.33: All the city was gathered together at the door.
web.34: He healed many who were sick with various diseases, and cast out many demons. He didn’t allow the demons to speak, because they knew him.
web.35: Early in the morning, while it was still dark, he rose up and went out, and departed into a deserted place, and prayed there.
web.36: Simon and those who were with him followed after him;
web.37: and they found him, and told him, “Everyone is looking for you.”
web.38: He said to them, “Let’s go elsewhere into the next towns, that I may preach there also, because I came out for this reason.”
web.39: He went into their synagogues throughout all Galilee, preaching and casting out demons.
web.40: A leper came to him, begging him, kneeling down to him, and saying to him, “If you want to, you can make me clean.”
web.41: Being moved with compassion, he stretched out his hand, and touched him, and said to him, “I want to. Be made clean.”
web.42: When he had said this, immediately the leprosy departed from him, and he was made clean.
web.43: He strictly warned him, and immediately sent him out,
web.44: and said to him, “See you say nothing to anybody, but go show yourself to the priest, and offer for your cleansing the things which Moses commanded, for a testimony to them.”
web.45: But he went out, and began to proclaim it much, and to spread about the matter, so that Jesus could no more openly enter into a city, but was outside in desert places: and they came to him from everywhere.

asv.1: The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God.
asv.2: Even as it is written in Isaiah the prophet, Behold, I send my messenger before thy face, Who shall prepare thy way.
asv.3: The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Make ye ready the way of the Lord, Make his paths straight;
asv.4: John came, who baptized in the wilderness and preached the baptism of repentance unto remission of sins.
asv.5: And there went out unto him all the country of Judaea, and all they of Jerusalem; And they were baptized of him in the river Jordan, confessing their sins.
asv.6: And John was clothed with camel`s hair, and [had] a leathern girdle about his loins, and did eat locusts and wild honey.
asv.7: And he preached, saying, There cometh after me he that is mightier than I, the latchet of whose shoes I am not worthy to stoop down and unloose.
asv.8: I baptized you in water; But he shall baptize you in the Holy Spirit.
asv.9: And it came to pass in those days, that Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee, and was baptized of John in the Jordan.
asv.10: And straightway coming up out of the water, he saw the heavens rent asunder, and the Spirit as a dove descending upon him:
asv.11: And a voice came out of the heavens, Thou art my beloved Son, in thee I am well pleased.
asv.12: And straightway the Spirit driveth him forth into the wilderness.
asv.13: And he was in the wilderness forty days tempted of Satan; And he was with the wild beasts; And the angels ministered unto him.
asv.14: Now after John was delivered up, Jesus came into Galilee, preaching the gospel of God,
asv.15: and saying, The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand: repent ye, and believe in the gospel.
asv.16: And passing along by the sea of Galilee, he saw Simon and Andrew the brother of Simon casting a net in the sea; for they were fishers.
asv.17: And Jesus said unto them, Come ye after me, and I will make you to become fishers of men.
asv.18: And straightway they left the nets, and followed him.
asv.19: And going on a little further, he saw James the [son] of Zebedee, and John his brother, who also were in the boat mending the nets.
asv.20: And straightway he called them: and they left their father Zebedee in the boat with the hired servants, and went after him.
asv.21: And they go into Capernaum; and straightway on the sabbath day he entered into the synagogue and taught.
asv.22: And they were astonished at his teaching: For he taught them as having authority, and not as the scribes.
asv.23: And straightway there was in their synagogue a man with an unclean spirit; and he cried out,
asv.24: saying, What have we to do with thee, Jesus thou Nazarene? art thou come to destroy us? I know thee who thou art, the Holy One of God.
asv.25: And Jesus rebuked him, saying, Hold thy peace, and come out of him.
asv.26: And the unclean spirit, tearing him and crying with a loud voice, came out of him.
asv.27: And they were all amazed, insomuch that they questioned among themselves, saying, What is this? a new teaching! with authority he commandeth even the unclean spirits, and they obey him.
asv.28: And the report of him went out straightway everywhere into all the region of Galilee round about.
asv.29: And straightway, when they were come out of the synagogue, they came into the house of Simon and Andrew, with James and John.
asv.30: Now Simon`s wife`s mother lay sick of a fever; and straightway they tell him of her:
asv.31: and he came and took her by the hand, and raised her up; and the fever left her, and she ministered unto them.
asv.32: And at even, when the sun did set, they brought unto him all that were sick, and them that were possessed with demons.
asv.33: And all the city was gathered together at the door.
asv.34: And he healed many that were sick with divers diseases, and cast out many demons; and he suffered not the demons to speak, because they knew him.
asv.35: And in the morning, a great while before day, he rose up and went out, and departed into a desert place, and there prayed.
asv.36: And Simon and they that were with him followed after him;
asv.37: and they found him, and say unto him, All are seeking thee.
asv.38: And he saith unto them, Let us go elsewhere into the next towns, that I may preach there also; for to this end came I forth.
asv.39: And he went into their synagogues throughout all Galilee, preaching and casting out demons.
asv.40: And there cometh to him a leper, beseeching him, and kneeling down to him, and saying unto him, If thou wilt, thou canst make me clean.
asv.41: And being moved with compassion, he stretched forth his hand, and touched him, and saith unto him, I will; be thou made clean.
asv.42: And straightway the leprosy departed from him, and he was made clean.
asv.43: And he strictly charged him, and straightway sent him out,
asv.44: and saith unto him, See thou say nothing to any man: but go show thyself to the priest, and offer for thy cleansing the things which Moses commanded, for a testimony unto them.
asv.45: But he went out, and began to publish it much, and to spread abroad the matter, insomuch that Jesus could no more openly enter into a city, but was without in desert places: and they came to him from every quarter.

kjv.1: The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God;
kjv.2: As it is written in the prophets, Behold, I send my messenger before thy face, which shall prepare thy way before thee.
kjv.3: The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord, make his paths straight.
kjv.4: John did baptize in the wilderness, and preach the baptism of repentance for the remission of sins.
kjv.5: And there went out unto him all the land of Judaea, and they of Jerusalem, and were all baptized of him in the river of Jordan, confessing their sins.
kjv.6: And John was clothed with camel’s hair, and with a girdle of a skin about his loins; and he did eat locusts and wild honey;
kjv.7: And preached, saying, There cometh one mightier than I after me, the latchet of whose shoes I am not worthy to stoop down and unloose.
kjv.8: I indeed have baptized you with water: but he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost.
kjv.9: And it came to pass in those days, that Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee, and was baptized of John in Jordan.
kjv.10: And straightway coming up out of the water, he saw the heavens opened, and the Spirit like a dove descending upon him:
kjv.11: And there came a voice from heaven, saying, Thou art my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.
kjv.12: And immediately the Spirit driveth him into the wilderness.
kjv.13: And he was there in the wilderness forty days, tempted of Satan; and was with the wild beasts; and the angels ministered unto him.
kjv.14: Now after that John was put in prison, Jesus came into Galilee, preaching the gospel of the kingdom of God,
kjv.15: And saying, The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand: repent ye, and believe the gospel.
kjv.16: Now as he walked by the sea of Galilee, he saw Simon and Andrew his brother casting a net into the sea: for they were fishers.
kjv.17: And Jesus said unto them, Come ye after me, and I will make you to become fishers of men.
kjv.18: And straightway they forsook their nets, and followed him.
kjv.19: And when he had gone a little farther thence, he saw James the son of Zebedee, and John his brother, who also were in the ship mending their nets.
kjv.20: And straightway he called them: and they left their father Zebedee in the ship with the hired servants, and went after him.
kjv.21: And they went into Capernaum; and straightway on the sabbath day he entered into the synagogue, and taught.
kjv.22: And they were astonished at his doctrine: for he taught them as one that had authority, and not as the scribes.
kjv.23: And there was in their synagogue a man with an unclean spirit; and he cried out,
kjv.24: Saying, Let us alone; what have we to do with thee, thou Jesus of Nazareth? art thou come to destroy us? I know thee who thou art, the Holy One of God.
kjv.25: And Jesus rebuked him, saying, Hold thy peace, and come out of him.
kjv.26: And when the unclean spirit had torn him, and cried with a loud voice, he came out of him.
kjv.27: And they were all amazed, insomuch that they questioned among themselves, saying, What thing is this? what new doctrine is this? for with authority commandeth he even the unclean spirits, and they do obey him.
kjv.28: And immediately his fame spread abroad throughout all the region round about Galilee.
kjv.29: And forthwith, when they were come out of the synagogue, they entered into the house of Simon and Andrew, with James and John.
kjv.30: But Simon’s wife’s mother lay sick of a fever, and anon they tell him of her.
kjv.31: And he came and took her by the hand, and lifted her up; and immediately the fever left her, and she ministered unto them.
kjv.32: And at even, when the sun did set, they brought unto him all that were diseased, and them that were possessed with devils.
kjv.33: And all the city was gathered together at the door.
kjv.34: And he healed many that were sick of divers diseases, and cast out many devils; and suffered not the devils to speak, because they knew him.
kjv.35: And in the morning, rising up a great while before day, he went out, and departed into a solitary place, and there prayed.
kjv.36: And Simon and they that were with him followed after him.
kjv.37: And when they had found him, they said unto him, All men seek for thee.
kjv.38: And he said unto them, Let us go into the next towns, that I may preach there also: for therefore came I forth.
kjv.39: And he preached in their synagogues throughout all Galilee, and cast out devils.
kjv.40: And there came a leper to him, beseeching him, and kneeling down to him, and saying unto him, If thou wilt, thou canst make me clean.
kjv.41: And Jesus, moved with compassion, put forth his hand, and touched him, and saith unto him, I will; be thou clean.
kjv.42: And as soon as he had spoken, immediately the leprosy departed from him, and he was cleansed.
kjv.43: And he straitly charged him, and forthwith sent him away;
kjv.44: And saith unto him, See thou say nothing to any man: but go thy way, shew thyself to the priest, and offer for thy cleansing those things which Moses commanded, for a testimony unto them.
kjv.45: But he went out, and began to publish it much, and to blaze abroad the matter, insomuch that Jesus could no more openly enter into the city, but was without in desert places: and they came to him from every quarter.

notes.title: Whose gospel? The genitive Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ
notes.body:
τοῦ εὐαγγελίου **Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ** — "the gospel *of Jesus Christ*." The genitive is genuinely ambiguous, and the ambiguity matters:

- **Objective genitive**: the gospel *about* Jesus Christ — Jesus is the content of the message. Fits Mark's book, which is precisely the story of Jesus.
- **Subjective genitive**: the gospel *proclaimed by* Jesus Christ — Jesus is the messenger. Fits 1:14–15, where Jesus himself preaches the gospel.

Most translations keep the ambiguous English "of," which is a legitimate translation *decision*: preserve the ambiguity rather than resolve it. Some commentators argue Mark intends both (a "plenary" genitive). If your translation must choose, note what you gain and lose either way.

notes.title: Is "Son of God" original?
notes.body:
υἱοῦ θεοῦ ("Son of God") is missing from some important early witnesses (notably the original hand of Codex Sinaiticus א*), and scholars are divided:

- **For omission being original**: the shorter reading is generally preferred; a scribe expanding a sacred title is more likely than one dropping it.
- **For inclusion being original**: the string of six genitives ending in nomina sacra abbreviations (ΙΥ ΧΥ ΥΥ ΘΥ) makes an accidental skip of the eye very easy; and "Son of God" frames Mark's whole narrative (1:11; 9:7; 15:39).

Most modern editions print it, some in brackets. A translation note ("some manuscripts omit *Son of God*") is the honest rendering of the uncertainty. This is a good first example of how the *text being translated* is itself a decision prior to translation.

notes.title: In Isaiah the prophet" — but the quote is composite
notes.body:
Mark attributes the quotation to **Isaiah**, but vv. 2–3 splice together three texts: Exodus 23:20 ("I send my messenger before your face"), Malachi 3:1 ("who will prepare your way"), and only then Isaiah 40:3 ("a voice crying in the wilderness…").

Two consequences for the translator:

1. **Textual variant**: the Byzantine text (behind the KJV) reads "in the **prophets**" — almost certainly a scribal smoothing of the difficulty. Compare KJV against ASV/WEB on this verse and you are looking at a text-critical decision, not a translation one.
2. **Don't fix it in translation**: rendering it "in the prophets" or footnoting it away removes a deliberate Markan move — naming Isaiah flags the *dominant* text of the composite and signals that the whole Gospel unfolds under Isaiah's "way of the LORD."

Note also ὁδός ("way") introduced here becomes a Markan keyword (8:27; 9:33–34; 10:32, 52).

notes.title: εἰς ἄφεσιν ἁμαρτιῶν — the force of εἰς
notes.body:
John proclaims "a baptism of repentance **εἰς** forgiveness of sins." The preposition εἰς with the accusative usually marks direction or goal, so the standard rendering is purposive: *"for / unto / leading to* the forgiveness of sins."

Watch what each option asserts:

- **"for the forgiveness of sins"** — forgiveness is the goal of the repentance-baptism (most translations).
- **"because of forgiveness"** — a causal εἰς has been proposed (mostly in debates about baptism's efficacy), but causal εἰς is doubtful Greek and special pleading here.

Also note the genitive chain: βάπτισμα **μετανοίας** is a "baptism *characterized by* repentance" (attributed/descriptive genitive) — not a baptism that produces repentance. English "a repentance-baptism" is ugly but shows the structure.

notes.title: εὐδόκησα — an aorist rendered as English present
notes.body:
The voice from heaven ends ἐν σοὶ **εὐδόκησα** — aorist, literally "I *was pleased* with you." Yet nearly every English version says "I **am** well pleased." Why?

The aorist here is best taken as **constative/timeless**: it sums up the Father's pleasure as a whole fact, without locating it at a past moment (some grammars compare the Hebrew stative perfect standing behind the idiom, cf. Isaiah 42:1). English simple past ("I was pleased") would wrongly imply the pleasure has ended.

This is a clean example of the golden rule of tense translation: **Greek tense encodes aspect; time comes from context.** Translating aorist mechanically as English past is the single most common beginner error.

notes.title: πεπλήρωται and ἤγγικεν — perfects with present force
notes.body:
Jesus' programmatic announcement uses two perfects: **πεπλήρωται** ὁ καιρός ("the time *has been fulfilled* — and now stands full") and **ἤγγικεν** ἡ βασιλεία ("the kingdom *has drawn near*").

The perfect asserts a completed event whose *result now holds*. That is exactly the theological freight here — the decisive moment has arrived and its effects are present.

ἤγγικεν is famously contested: does "has come near" mean *arrived* (C. H. Dodd's "realized eschatology": translate "has come") or *approached, is at hand* (imminent but not yet)? Compare the versions: KJV/ASV "is at hand," many modern versions "has come near." Your rendering of one Greek verb takes a position in a century-old scholarly debate — a vivid case of why translation justification matters.

notes.title: μετανοεῖτε καὶ πιστεύετε — present imperatives
notes.body:
Both commands are **present** imperatives, carrying imperfective (ongoing) aspect: not "make a one-off decision" but *"repent and keep believing"* — a summons to a continuing stance. English imperatives can't show this without paraphrase; most translations leave it invisible.

Also note πιστεύετε **ἐν** τῷ εὐαγγελίῳ: πιστεύω with ἐν is rare (classical Greek uses the dative or εἰς). It may reflect Semitic idiom (Hebrew הֶאֱמִין בְּ). "Believe **in** the gospel" is fine English, but be aware you are translating an unusual construction, not the ordinary one.

notes.title: Compassion or anger? σπλαγχνισθείς vs ὀργισθείς
notes.body:
Most manuscripts read **σπλαγχνισθείς** ("moved with compassion"), but Codex Bezae (D) and some Old Latin witnesses read **ὀργισθείς** ("moved with anger") — and many text critics take the harder reading seriously: why would a scribe change "compassion" *to* "anger," while softening "anger" to "compassion" is easy to imagine? (Note Mark 3:5 has no problem attributing anger to Jesus, and Matthew/Luke both drop the emotion word entirely — as if their copy of Mark had something awkward.)

- NIV (2011) actually translates "Jesus was **indignant**."
- Most versions stay with "moved with compassion/pity."

Whichever you print, this verse is the standard classroom example of the text-critical principle *lectio difficilior potior* ("the harder reading is stronger") — and of its limits, since the external evidence for σπλαγχνισθείς is overwhelming.
